options.explanation: Correct. Storage Explorer browses blobs, files, queues, and tables visually.
Wrong. Monitor is observability, not storage browsing.
Wrong. Locks prevent changes.
Wrong. NSGs filter traffic.

questions.stem: Which feature lets you verify whether traffic is allowed between two endpoints in a VNet?

options.text: IP flow verify in Network Watcher
options.explanation: Correct. IP flow verify checks if a packet is allowed or denied.
Wrong. Locks prevent changes.
Wrong. Tags are metadata.
Wrong. Unrelated to flow verification.

questions.stem: A new employee needs to manage all resources in one resource group but nothing elsewhere in the subscription. What is the least-privilege assignment?

options.text: Contributor role scoped to that resource group
options.explanation: Correct. RBAC is scope-bound; assign at the RG scope: `az role assignment create --assignee user@contoso.com --role Contributor --resource-group rg-app`.
Wrong. Grants full control over everything plus access management — far beyond the requirement.
Wrong. Correct role, wrong scope — they could change every RG.

options.text: Reader scoped to the resource group
options.explanation: Wrong. Reader can view but not manage resources.

questions.stem: You must prevent anyone — including Owners — from accidentally deleting a critical resource group. What do you apply?

options.text: A CanNotDelete management lock on the resource group
options.explanation: Correct. Locks override RBAC: `az lock create --name noDelete --lock-type CanNotDelete --resource-group rg-prod`. Even Owners cannot delete until the lock is removed.

options.text: Remove delete permission via a custom role
options.explanation: Wrong. Owners can reassign roles; a lock is the durable guard.

options.text: An Azure Policy with deny effect
options.explanation: Wrong. Policy governs property/SKU compliance, not deletion protection of an existing RG.
Wrong. Tags are metadata only; they enforce nothing.

questions.stem: Azure Policy should automatically add a 'costCenter' tag to new resources that lack it, inheriting from the resource group. Which policy effect do you need?

options.text: modify (with a remediation task for existing resources)
options.explanation: Correct. `modify` adds/updates tags on create/update; run `az policy remediation create` to tag existing resources retroactively.
Wrong. deny blocks non-compliant creates rather than fixing the tag.
Wrong. audit only flags non-compliance in the log; it changes nothing.
Wrong. append adds fields but cannot reference the RG tag for inheritance the way modify can; modify is the documented pattern for tag inheritance.

questions.stem: A guest user from another tenant needs access to a shared resource. How do you add them?

options.text: Invite them as a B2B guest in Microsoft Entra ID
options.explanation: Correct. `az ad user invite` (or the portal) creates a guest object; then assign RBAC normally. The user authenticates with their home tenant.

options.text: Create a new member user with their email
options.explanation: Wrong. That creates a separate identity they must manage a new password for; B2B reuses their existing identity.
Wrong. Never — violates least privilege and auditing.

options.text: Add them to an on-prem AD group
options.explanation: Wrong. On-prem groups don't grant Azure RBAC without sync and an Entra identity.

questions.stem: You want users to request elevated access only when needed, with approval and a time limit, instead of standing Owner rights. Which feature?

options.explanation: Correct. PIM makes roles eligible rather than active; users activate just-in-time with optional approval and expiry, reducing standing privilege.
Wrong. CA gates sign-in conditions, not role activation lifecycle.
Wrong. Reviews periodically recertify access but don't provide JIT activation.
Wrong. Still grants standing access whenever assigned.

questions.stem: A storage account must reject all traffic except from one specific VNet subnet. What configuration achieves this?

options.text: Set default network action to Deny, then add the subnet as a network rule
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage account update --name st --default-action Deny` then `az storage account network-rule add --subnet <id>`. Default-deny plus an allow rule for the subnet.
Wrong. Valid alternative, but with the public endpoint still default-Allow other networks reach it; the question asks to reject all except the subnet via firewall rules.

options.text: Set default action to Allow and add the subnet
options.explanation: Wrong. Allow leaves every other network permitted.
Wrong. Locks are about deletion, not network access.

questions.stem: Blobs in a container must move to Cool tier after 30 days and be deleted after 365 days automatically. What do you configure?

options.text: A lifecycle management policy on the storage account
options.explanation: Correct. Define a JSON rule with tierToCool afterModificationGreaterThan 30 and delete after 365, applied via `az storage account management-policy create`.

options.text: Manually re-tier with a scheduled runbook
options.explanation: Wrong. Reinvents built-in lifecycle management and is error-prone.
Wrong. Replicates blobs across accounts; not a tiering/expiry tool.

options.text: Change the account's default access tier
options.explanation: Wrong. Sets a single default, not time-based transitions per blob.

questions.stem: You need to grant a partner temporary read access to a single blob without sharing the account key. What do you generate?

options.text: A user delegation SAS for that blob
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage blob generate-sas --auth-mode login --as-user --permissions r --expiry ...` signs with Entra credentials, scoped and time-limited, no account key exposed.
Wrong. Grants full account access and can't be scoped or expired easily.

options.text: A stored access policy with write
options.explanation: Wrong. Write exceeds 'read', violating least privilege.

options.text: Anonymous public read on the container
options.explanation: Wrong. Exposes the blob to everyone, not just the partner.

questions.stem: An Azure File share must be accessible from on-prem Windows machines over SMB without opening port 445 to the internet. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: A private endpoint for the file share plus VPN/ExpressRoute connectivity
options.explanation: Correct. Private endpoint gives the share a private IP reached over the VPN tunnel, avoiding public 445 which ISPs often block. `az network private-endpoint create ...`.

options.text: Expose the public endpoint and allow 445
options.explanation: Wrong. Many ISPs block 445 and it's a security risk.
Wrong. Sync caches to a server but doesn't itself solve direct SMB reachability requirement.
Wrong. AzCopy is a copy tool, not a mounted SMB share.

questions.stem: A running VM needs a larger size, but the target SKU isn't offered while the VM is allocated. What is the correct procedure?

options.text: Deallocate the VM, then resize, then start it
options.explanation: Correct. `az vm deallocate` frees the host, `az vm resize --size Standard_D4s_v5` allows SKUs not on the current host, then `az vm start`.

options.text: Resize live; Azure always allows any SKU
options.explanation: Wrong. Only sizes available on the current host cluster work without deallocation.
Wrong. Unnecessary and loses ephemeral state; resize is supported.

options.text: Add a second NIC to increase size
options.explanation: Wrong. NICs don't change compute size.

questions.stem: You need three VMs to remain available during both unplanned hardware failures and planned maintenance, within a single datacenter. What do you use?

options.text: An availability set with multiple fault and update domains
options.explanation: Correct. Fault domains span racks/power; update domains stagger maintenance reboots — protecting against both within one datacenter. Create at VM provisioning time.
Wrong. Zones protect across datacenters (higher SLA) but the requirement specifies a single datacenter; either is defensible, yet availability set is the single-DC construct.

options.text: A scale set with one instance
options.explanation: Wrong. One instance gives no redundancy.

options.text: Three VMs in the same fault domain
options.explanation: Wrong. A single fault domain shares failure risk.

questions.stem: A VM scale set must add instances when average CPU exceeds 70% for 10 minutes and remove them below 30%. What do you configure?

options.text: Autoscale rules on the scale set tied to the CPU metric
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor autoscale create` + `az monitor autoscale rule create --condition "Percentage CPU > 70 avg 10m" --scale out 1`, plus a scale-in rule at 30%.
Wrong. Vertical scaling doesn't auto-adjust to load.
Wrong. Doesn't meet the automatic requirement.
Wrong. Probes route traffic; they don't change instance count.

questions.stem: You must run a containerized web app without managing any VMs or orchestrators, scaling to zero when idle. Which compute service fits best?

options.text: Azure Container Apps (or Container Instances) — serverless containers
options.explanation: Correct. Container Apps runs containers serverlessly with scale-to-zero and KEDA-based autoscaling; no node management. `az containerapp create ...`.
Wrong. AKS requires managing node pools and the cluster.
Wrong. You manage the VM, patching, and scaling yourself.

options.text: App Service on an Isolated plan
options.explanation: Wrong. Heavier and doesn't scale to zero the way the requirement implies.

questions.stem: A VM's OS disk is corrupted and it won't boot. You need to inspect the disk from another VM. What is the standard recovery step?

options.text: Swap the OS disk or attach it as a data disk to a repair VM
options.explanation: Correct. `az vm repair create` builds a repair VM with the problem disk attached, or `az vm update --os-disk` swaps in a fixed disk. Mount and fix offline.
Wrong. Reimage on a managed-disk VM may lose data; inspect first.
Wrong. Size is unrelated to a corrupt boot disk.
Wrong. Diagnostics help diagnose but don't repair the disk.

questions.stem: Two VNets in the same region must communicate privately with low latency, but transitive routing through a third VNet is not required. What do you configure?

options.text: VNet peering between the two VNets
options.explanation: Correct. `az network vnet peering create` links them over the Microsoft backbone with private IPs and low latency. Peering is non-transitive by default.
Wrong. Works but adds latency, cost, and throughput limits versus peering for same-region VNets.
Wrong. Subnets can't span VNets.
Wrong. Service endpoints secure access to PaaS services, not VNet-to-VNet connectivity.

questions.stem: An NSG allows inbound 443 but users still can't reach the VM's web app on 443. The VM's local firewall is open. What should you check next?

options.text: Whether a higher-priority NSG rule denies the traffic, and effective security rules
options.explanation: Correct. NSG rules are evaluated by priority (lower number first). `az network nic list-effective-nsg` shows the merged subnet+NIC rules and which rule actually wins.
Wrong. Size is unrelated to connectivity.
Wrong. If one exists, re-adding doesn't fix a deny rule.
Wrong. Doesn't address rule precedence.

questions.stem: Web servers in a private subnet must download OS updates from the internet but must not be reachable inbound from the internet. What do you deploy?

options.text: A NAT gateway on the subnet for outbound connectivity
options.explanation: Correct. `az network nat gateway create` then associate to the subnet — provides SNAT for outbound only, with no inbound exposure and no public IPs on the VMs.

options.text: A public IP on each VM
options.explanation: Wrong. Public IPs allow inbound exposure, the opposite of the requirement.
Wrong. That's for inbound traffic distribution.
Wrong. Service endpoints reach Azure PaaS, not arbitrary internet update servers.

questions.stem: You need name resolution for VMs across several peered VNets using custom domain names, without managing DNS servers. What do you use?

options.text: An Azure Private DNS zone linked to the VNets with autoregistration
options.explanation: Correct. `az network private-dns zone create` + `az network private-dns link vnet create --registration-enabled true` auto-registers VM records and resolves across linked VNets.
Wrong. Default DNS doesn't resolve custom names across VNets.
Wrong. Public zones expose records to the internet and don't autoregister private VMs.
Wrong. Unmanageable at scale and not dynamic.

questions.stem: Traffic to your global web app should route users to the nearest healthy regional endpoint at the DNS level. Which service do you choose?

options.text: Azure Traffic Manager with performance routing
options.explanation: Correct. Traffic Manager is DNS-based global routing; performance method directs users to the lowest-latency endpoint and fails over on health-probe failures.
Wrong. Regional L4 only — not global DNS routing.
Wrong. Regional L7; pair it behind Front Door/Traffic Manager for global.
Wrong. No geographic routing or failover.

questions.stem: You must be alerted when a VM's average CPU exceeds 80% over 5 minutes. What do you create?

options.text: A metric alert rule on the Percentage CPU signal with an action group
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor metrics alert create --condition "avg Percentage CPU > 80" --window-size 5m --action <ag>` notifies via the action group (email/SMS/webhook).

options.text: A log query alert on heartbeat
options.explanation: Wrong. Heartbeat tracks availability, not CPU thresholds.
Wrong. Captures console output, not metric alerts.
Wrong. Budgets track cost, not CPU.

questions.stem: You need to centralize and query logs from many VMs and run KQL across them. What do you set up?

options.text: A Log Analytics workspace with the Azure Monitor Agent and a data collection rule
options.explanation: Correct. Install AMA, define a DCR to collect logs, and query with KQL in the workspace: `az monitor log-analytics workspace create` then associate via DCR.
Wrong. Storage archives logs but offers no KQL query engine.
Wrong. Per-VM console only.
Wrong. Network Watcher is for network diagnostics, not general VM log querying.

questions.stem: Diagnostic settings on a resource should stream platform logs to three destinations at once: archive, SIEM, and analytics. Which three are valid targets?

options.text: Storage account, Event Hub, and Log Analytics workspace
options.explanation: Correct. Diagnostic settings support all three simultaneously — storage for archive, Event Hub for SIEM ingestion, Log Analytics for KQL. `az monitor diagnostic-settings create`.
Wrong. The requirement names three destinations.
Wrong. Single target doesn't meet the requirement.
Wrong. Disks aren't diagnostic-settings targets.

questions.stem: After a VM was accidentally deleted, you must recover its data disk that was retained. The disk still exists as a managed disk. How do you bring the VM back?

options.text: Create a new VM and attach the retained managed disk as the OS disk
options.explanation: Correct. Managed disks persist independently. `az vm create --attach-os-disk <diskName>` rebuilds the VM from the surviving disk.
Wrong. Azure has no generic VM undelete; you rebuild from the disk.
Wrong. Diagnostics aren't a backup.
Wrong. Unrelated to recovery.

questions.stem: An Azure Backup must protect a VM with a 7-year retention for monthly points. Where do you configure this?

options.text: A Recovery Services vault with a backup policy specifying monthly retention of 7 years
options.explanation: Correct. `az backup policy set` defines daily/weekly/monthly/yearly retention; associate the VM via `az backup protection enable-for-vm`.
Wrong. That's for blobs, not VM backups.
Wrong. A single snapshot isn't a retention-managed backup.
Wrong. ASR is for replication/DR failover, not long-term backup retention.

questions.stem: A subscription must block creation of any resource outside the 'eastus' and 'westus' regions. What enforces this?

options.text: An Azure Policy with the 'Allowed locations' built-in definition and deny effect
options.explanation: Correct. Assign the built-in allowed-locations policy listing the two regions: `az policy assignment create --policy <allowed-locations> --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","westus"]}}'`.
Wrong. RBAC governs who, not which region resources may use.
Wrong. Locks prevent changes/deletes, not region selection.
Wrong. Tags don't enforce constraints.

questions.stem: You must apply the same VM configuration (extensions, tags, NIC settings) repeatably across environments via declarative IaC native to Azure. What do you use?

options.text: An ARM/Bicep template deployed with az deployment
options.explanation: Correct. `az deployment group create --template-file main.bicep` deploys declarative, idempotent infrastructure — repeatable across environments.
Wrong. Not repeatable or version-controlled.

options.text: A shell script of az commands with no state
options.explanation: Wrong. Imperative scripts drift and aren't idempotent like templates.
Wrong. Images capture the OS, not the surrounding resource configuration.

questions.stem: An Application Gateway must route /api/* to one backend pool and /images/* to another. What feature do you configure?

options.text: Path-based routing rules on the Application Gateway
options.explanation: Correct. URL path maps send /api/* and /images/* to different backend pools on the same listener: `az network application-gateway url-path-map create ...`.
Wrong. NSGs filter by IP/port, not URL path.
Wrong. DNS-level, can't inspect URL paths.
Wrong. Doesn't provide path-based L7 routing on one endpoint.

questions.stem: You need to copy 5 TB of blobs between two storage accounts efficiently from a VM. Which tool is recommended?

options.text: AzCopy with a SAS or Entra auth
options.explanation: Correct. `azcopy copy 'https://src...' 'https://dst...' --recursive` is optimized for high-throughput bulk transfer between accounts.
Wrong. Impractical for 5 TB.

options.text: az storage blob download then upload one by one
options.explanation: Wrong. Slow and serial versus AzCopy's parallelism.

options.text: Object replication for a one-time copy
options.explanation: Wrong. Replication is for ongoing async sync, not a single bulk move.

questions.stem: Self-service password reset (SSPR) should be enabled for a pilot group only, not the whole tenant. Where do you scope it?

options.text: In Entra ID SSPR settings, set it to a selected security group
options.explanation: Correct. SSPR can target 'Selected' and a security group; add pilot users to the group rather than enabling for 'All'.
Wrong. CA gates sign-in; it doesn't toggle SSPR scope.
Wrong. RBAC is Azure resource access, not SSPR.
Wrong. Licensing enables capability but scoping is done via the SSPR group setting.

questions.stem: You must capture and analyze packets to/from a VM to troubleshoot a connectivity issue. Which tool do you use?

options.explanation: Correct. `az network watcher packet-capture create` records traffic on the VM to a storage account for analysis with Wireshark.
Wrong. Console output, not packet data.
Wrong. Availability signal, not packets.
Wrong. Flow logs summarize allow/deny by 5-tuple but don't capture full packets.

questions.stem: VMs in a subnet must reach Azure Storage over the Microsoft backbone, keeping traffic off the public internet, without per-resource private endpoints. What's the simplest fit?

options.text: A service endpoint for Microsoft.Storage on the subnet
options.explanation: Correct. `az network vnet subnet update --service-endpoints Microsoft.Storage` routes storage traffic over the backbone and lets the storage firewall trust the subnet.

options.text: A private endpoint per storage account
options.explanation: Wrong. Works but the question asks to avoid per-resource endpoints; service endpoint covers the subnet.
Wrong. NAT is outbound internet, not backbone access to PaaS.
Wrong. Sends traffic on-prem, the opposite goal.

questions.stem: Dev VMs should automatically shut down at 7 PM daily to save cost. What's the built-in way to configure this?

options.text: The VM's Auto-shutdown setting (DevTest Labs schedule)
options.explanation: Correct. Each VM has an Auto-shutdown blade; set the time and time zone. CLI: `az vm auto-shutdown -g rg -n vm --time 1900`.
Wrong. Locks don't power off VMs.
Wrong. Alerts notify but don't stop the VM.

options.text: Resize to a smaller SKU nightly
options.explanation: Wrong. Doesn't stop billing for compute the way deallocation does.

questions.stem: A storage account must be reachable only through a private IP inside the VNet, with no public endpoint at all. What do you deploy and disable?

options.text: A private endpoint, and set public network access to Disabled
options.explanation: Correct. `az network private-endpoint create ...` plus `az storage account update --public-network-access Disabled` removes the public endpoint entirely.
Wrong. Service endpoints still use the storage public IP; they don't give a private IP.
Wrong. The public endpoint still exists, merely firewalled.
Wrong. Unrelated to inbound private access.

questions.stem: You need all administrators to use MFA when accessing the Azure portal, but not when on the corporate network. What enforces this?

options.text: A Conditional Access policy requiring MFA, excluding the trusted named location
options.explanation: Correct. CA targets the admin roles, requires MFA, and excludes the corporate IP named location: `Grant: require MFA`, `Conditions: exclude trusted locations`.
Wrong. Legacy per-user MFA can't express the network exclusion condition.
Wrong. Security defaults are all-or-nothing and not location-aware.
Wrong. Unrelated to authentication.

questions.stem: Two VMs behind a public Standard Load Balancer can't receive traffic though the LB rule exists. The backend health probe shows unhealthy. What's the most likely fix?

options.text: Ensure the NSG allows the probe source (AzureLoadBalancer) and the app listens on the probe port
options.explanation: Correct. Standard LB requires explicit NSG allow; the probe comes from the AzureLoadBalancer service tag and the app must answer on the probed port for instances to be marked healthy.

options.text: Switch to a Basic load balancer
options.explanation: Wrong. Basic is being retired and doesn't fix a probe/NSG issue.

options.text: Add public IPs to the VMs
options.explanation: Wrong. Backend VMs behind an LB don't need their own public IPs.
Wrong. Unrelated to health probes.

questions.stem: You want a single dashboard showing CPU, disk, and network for a fleet of VMs with prebuilt charts and no manual KQL. What do you enable?

options.explanation: Correct. VM insights deploys the agent and a dependency map plus prebuilt performance workbooks across the fleet — enable via the Monitor > Insights blade.
Wrong. Per-VM console only.
Wrong. Archives logs without dashboards.
Wrong. Shows network topology, not VM performance dashboards.

questions.stem: An App Service web app needs to access a Key Vault secret without storing credentials. What do you configure?

options.text: A managed identity on the App Service with a Key Vault access policy/RBAC
options.explanation: Correct. Enable system-assigned identity (`az webapp identity assign`), grant it `get` on secrets, and reference the secret — no credentials in code or config.

options.text: The Key Vault access key in app settings
options.explanation: Wrong. Key Vault has no shared 'access key'; storing secrets defeats the purpose.

options.text: A service principal secret in code
options.explanation: Wrong. That's a stored credential — the thing we're avoiding.
Wrong. Key Vault doesn't allow anonymous access.

questions.stem: You must ensure a storage account's data survives a full regional outage with automatic read access from the secondary region. Which redundancy option?

options.explanation: Correct. RA-GRS replicates to a paired region and the -RA option exposes a readable secondary endpoint during a primary outage. `az storage account update --sku Standard_RAGRS`.
Wrong. LRS stays in one datacenter — no regional protection.
Wrong. ZRS spans zones in one region, not across regions.
Wrong. GRS protects data but the secondary isn't readable until failover; the requirement needs read access.

questions.stem: On-prem network 10.1.0.0/16 must connect to an Azure VNet over an encrypted tunnel across the internet. What do you deploy in Azure?

options.text: A VPN gateway with a site-to-site connection and local network gateway
options.explanation: Correct. `az network vnet-gateway create` (VpnGw SKU) + a local network gateway describing on-prem, joined by an IPsec connection over the public internet.
Wrong. ExpressRoute is a private circuit, not 'across the internet'; also far costlier for this requirement.
Wrong. Peering is Azure-to-Azure, not on-prem.
Wrong. Outbound SNAT only, no on-prem tunnel.

questions.stem: Costs must be attributed per department. You need spending visibility grouped by a 'department' tag and a budget alert at 80% per group. What combination do you use?

options.text: Cost Management with tag-based grouping plus a budget with an 80% alert threshold
options.explanation: Correct. Apply the department tag (enforce via policy), group in Cost analysis by that tag, and `az consumption budget create` with an 80% notification.
Wrong. Doesn't track or alert on cost.
Wrong. Controls access, not cost reporting.
Wrong. Those fire on control-plane operations, not spend thresholds.

questions.stem: You must deploy 50 identical VMs that scale together and share a load balancer, managed as one resource. What do you use?

options.explanation: Correct. VMSS manages a group of identical VMs with integrated load balancing and autoscale as a single resource: `az vmss create --instance-count 50 ...`.

options.text: 50 individual VMs in an availability set
options.explanation: Wrong. Not managed as one unit and lacks integrated autoscale.
Wrong. PaaS web hosting, not raw VM fleet management.
Wrong. Kubernetes nodes, a different abstraction than identical IaaS VMs.

questions.stem: An alert should trigger a remediation automatically (e.g., restart a service) when fired, not just notify. What do you attach to the action group?

options.text: An automation runbook or Azure Function as an action group action
options.explanation: Correct. Action groups can call an Automation runbook, Function, Logic App, or webhook to remediate automatically when the alert fires.
Wrong. Email notifies but cannot remediate.

options.text: A metric alert with no action group
options.explanation: Wrong. Without an action nothing happens.
Wrong. Routes logs, doesn't run remediation.

questions.stem: You need an immutable, write-once-read-many policy on a blob container for compliance. What do you apply?

options.text: A time-based or legal-hold immutability policy on the container
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage container immutability-policy create` enforces WORM — blobs can't be modified or deleted for the retention period.
Wrong. Locks act on the resource, not blob-level WORM compliance.
Wrong. Soft delete enables recovery, not immutability.
Wrong. That tiers/expires blobs rather than locking them.

questions.stem: Several subscriptions should share one set of policies and RBAC assignments applied at a level above subscriptions. What do you use?

options.explanation: Correct. Place subscriptions under a management group; assign policy and RBAC once at the group and they inherit downward. `az account management-group create`.
Wrong. RGs live inside a single subscription.
Wrong. Tags don't apply policy or RBAC.
Wrong. Doesn't address governing multiple existing subscriptions.

questions.stem: App Gateway must terminate TLS and protect the app from common web exploits like SQL injection. What do you enable?

options.text: Application Gateway WAF SKU with the OWASP rule set
options.explanation: Correct. The WAF v2 SKU terminates TLS and inspects requests against OWASP CRS, blocking SQLi/XSS. `az network application-gateway waf-policy create`.
Wrong. NSGs filter by IP/port, not application-layer attacks.
Wrong. Helpful for network filtering but WAF is the app-layer exploit protection paired with App Gateway.
Wrong. Mitigates volumetric attacks, not SQL injection.

questions.stem: A VM must join the domain and install monitoring automatically right after provisioning, declaratively. What mechanism do you use?

options.text: VM extensions (e.g., custom script / domain join / AMA) in the deployment template
options.explanation: Correct. Extensions run post-provision tasks; declare them in Bicep/ARM or `az vm extension set` to domain-join and install agents idempotently.
Wrong. Not declarative or repeatable.
Wrong. Unrelated to post-deploy configuration.
Wrong. Captures disk state, not a configuration workflow.

questions.stem: You must retain activity (control-plane) logs for the subscription for 2 years for audit. What do you configure?

options.text: A diagnostic setting on the subscription Activity Log exporting to a storage account or Log Analytics with retention
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor diagnostic-settings subscription create` ships the Activity Log to storage (set lifecycle/retention) or a workspace for 2-year audit retention.
Wrong. Per-VM console only.
Wrong. Alerts don't retain logs.
Wrong. Those are network flow records, not control-plane activity logs.

questions.stem: An AKS or VM workload needs a shared, concurrently mounted ReadWriteMany file system over SMB/NFS. Which Azure storage service fits?

options.text: Azure Files (Premium) mounted by multiple clients
options.explanation: Correct. Azure Files supports concurrent SMB/NFS mounts (RWX). Create the share and mount it on each node; Premium tier for low latency.
Wrong. Managed disks attach to one VM at a time (RWO), not shared concurrent mounts.
Wrong. Object storage, not a mountable POSIX/SMB file system for RWX.
Wrong. Point-in-time copy, not a live shared mount.

questions.stem: A blob container must allow anonymous read of blobs but not list the container contents. Which public access level do you set?

options.text: Blob (anonymous read access for blobs only)
options.explanation: Correct. The 'Blob' level allows reading individual blobs by URL but not enumerating the container. `az storage container set-permission --public-access blob` (account must permit anonymous access).
Wrong. That also allows listing the container's blobs.
Wrong. Private blocks all anonymous read.
Wrong. SAS is scoped credentialed access, not anonymous public read.

questions.stem: Two VMs must have the lowest possible network latency between them for an HPC workload. Which placement construct do you use?

options.explanation: Correct. `az ppg create` then deploy both VMs into it to co-locate them physically, minimizing inter-VM latency.
Wrong. Zones increase resilience but add latency by separating datacenters.
Wrong. Isolates hardware but doesn't guarantee minimal inter-VM latency.
Wrong. Spreads across fault domains, which can increase latency.

questions.stem: You assigned Reader at the subscription and Contributor at a resource group to the same user. What can they do inside that resource group?

options.text: Contributor actions — the more specific/permissive applicable role applies, and RBAC is additive
options.explanation: Correct. RBAC permissions are the union of all assignments; within the RG the user has Contributor (read+write), inheriting Reader elsewhere.
Wrong. Assignments are additive, not most-restrictive, unless a deny assignment exists.
Wrong. Roles never cancel; they combine.
Wrong. Neither assignment grants access-management rights.

questions.stem: A custom role must allow restarting VMs but nothing else. Which actions belong in the role definition?

options.explanation: Correct. `az role definition create` with Actions limited to the restart operation and the read needed to see the VM enforces least privilege.
Wrong. Wildcard grants all compute operations, far beyond restart.
Wrong. Read can't restart.
Wrong. That's access management, unrelated and dangerous.

questions.stem: You need to mount an Azure File share on a Linux VM. Which protocol and command pattern apply?

options.text: SMB (or NFS) mount using the storage account key or identity
options.explanation: Correct. e.g. `mount -t cifs //acct.file.core.windows.net/share /mnt/share -o credentials=...`; get the key via `az storage account keys list`.
Wrong. Blob isn't a mountable file system.
Wrong. Azure Files doesn't expose FTP.
Wrong. Azure Files uses SMB/NFS, not iSCSI.

questions.stem: A scale set deployment must update instances gradually, keeping most online during a new image rollout. Which upgrade policy do you set?

options.text: Rolling upgrade policy with a max batch and pause between batches
options.explanation: Correct. `az vmss update --set upgradePolicy.mode=Rolling` updates instances in batches honoring maxBatchInstancePercent and health checks, keeping the rest serving.

options.text: Manual upgrade with all at once
options.explanation: Wrong. Updating all instances simultaneously drops availability.

options.text: Delete and recreate the scale set
options.explanation: Wrong. Destroys the running service.
Wrong. Without health gating a bad image rolls out fully.

questions.stem: Peering exists between VNet-A and VNet-B, and between VNet-B and VNet-C. VMs in A cannot reach C. Why?

options.text: VNet peering is non-transitive; A↔C needs its own peering or a hub with routing
options.explanation: Correct. Spoke-to-spoke traffic doesn't transit a peered hub by default; add direct peering or route through an NVA/Azure Firewall in a hub-spoke design.
Wrong. It is not transitive by default.
Wrong. The structural cause is non-transitivity, not an NSG.
Wrong. The reachability gap is routing, not name resolution.

questions.stem: In a hub-spoke topology, all spoke traffic must be inspected by Azure Firewall in the hub. What do you configure on the spokes?

options.text: User-defined routes (UDR) with a 0.0.0.0/0 next hop of the firewall's private IP
options.explanation: Correct. `az network route-table route create --next-hop-type VirtualAppliance --next-hop-ip-address <fw>` forces spoke egress through the firewall.
Wrong. Those reach PaaS directly, bypassing the firewall.
Wrong. NSGs filter but don't redirect traffic to the firewall.
Wrong. Default routes send traffic straight out, not via the firewall.

questions.stem: You must rotate the storage account access keys without downtime for apps using key1. What's the safe procedure?

options.text: Switch apps to key2, regenerate key1, then switch back as needed
options.explanation: Correct. Two keys exist for zero-downtime rotation: move clients to key2 (`az storage account keys list`), `az storage account keys renew --key key1`, then rotate again later.
Wrong. Invalidates all clients simultaneously — downtime.
Wrong. Destructive and unnecessary.
Wrong. Breaks apps that depend on key auth.

questions.stem: An App Service should deploy from a staging slot and swap to production with zero downtime and easy rollback. What do you use?

options.text: Deployment slots with a swap operation
options.explanation: Correct. Deploy to 'staging', warm it up, then `az webapp deployment slot swap` performs a near-instant VIP swap; swap back to roll back.

options.text: Restart the production app after each deploy
options.explanation: Wrong. Causes downtime and no rollback.

options.text: A second App Service plan manually
options.explanation: Wrong. Reinvents slots without the integrated swap.
Wrong. Unrelated to deployment strategy.

questions.stem: Guest users must be reviewed quarterly and removed if no longer needed, automatically prompting reviewers. What do you configure?

options.text: Entra ID Access Reviews on the guest population
options.explanation: Correct. Access reviews recur (e.g., quarterly), email reviewers, and can auto-remove access for users who aren't recertified.
Wrong. PIM handles JIT role activation, not recurring recertification of guests.
Wrong. CA gates sign-in conditions, not periodic review.
Wrong. Unrelated to identity lifecycle.

questions.stem: You need a KQL query showing the top 5 VMs by CPU over the last hour from Log Analytics. Which table do you query?

options.text: Perf (or InsightsMetrics) filtered to the Processor counter, summarized and sorted
options.explanation: Correct. e.g. `Perf | where CounterName == "% Processor Time" | summarize avg(CounterValue) by Computer | top 5 by avg_CounterValue`.
Wrong. Tracks agent availability, not CPU.
Wrong. Control-plane operations, not performance counters.
Wrong. Wrong resource type/metric.

questions.stem: A VM must have a static private IP that never changes across reboots and deallocations. How do you set it?

options.text: Set the NIC's private IP allocation to Static
options.explanation: Correct. `az network nic ip-config update --set privateIpAllocationMethod=Static --private-ip-address 10.0.0.10` pins it; dynamic IPs can change on deallocation.
Wrong. That's the public address, not the private one.
Wrong. Azure doesn't expose DHCP reservations; you set allocation on the NIC.
Wrong. Doesn't pin the existing private IP.

questions.stem: Your AKS cluster needs to pull images from a private Azure Container Registry without storing credentials. What do you configure?

options.text: Attach the ACR to the cluster (managed identity AcrPull)
options.explanation: Correct. `az aks update --attach-acr <registry>` grants the cluster's identity AcrPull — image pulls work with no imagePullSecrets.

options.text: A docker login in each pod
options.explanation: Wrong. Stores credentials and isn't scalable.
Wrong. Defeats privacy/security.
Wrong. Unmanageable and bypasses the registry.

questions.stem: You must give a Logic App read access to a storage queue using its identity. What two steps are required?

options.text: Enable a managed identity on the Logic App, then assign it the Storage Queue Data Reader role
options.explanation: Correct. `az logicapp identity assign` then `az role assignment create --role "Storage Queue Data Reader" --assignee <principalId> --scope <queue>`.

options.text: Put the account key in the workflow
options.explanation: Wrong. Stored credential; avoid.
Wrong. Queues can't be anonymous and it's insecure.

options.text: Use a SAS hardcoded in the app
options.explanation: Wrong. Expiring secret in code; identity+RBAC is preferred.

questions.stem: Security defaults are enabled but you need granular control — MFA only for admins and risky sign-ins. What must you do first?

options.text: Disable security defaults and create Conditional Access policies
options.explanation: Correct. Security defaults and CA are mutually exclusive; turn defaults off, then author targeted CA policies (requires a P1/P2 license).

options.text: Keep security defaults and add CA on top
options.explanation: Wrong. You can't run both simultaneously.
Wrong. Lacks risk-based and granular targeting.
Wrong. Unrelated to authentication.

questions.stem: ExpressRoute is the primary on-prem link; you need an automatic failover path if the circuit goes down. What do you add?

options.text: A site-to-site VPN as a backup connection with appropriate routing/weights
options.explanation: Correct. Configure S2S VPN over the internet as failover; BGP/route weighting prefers ExpressRoute and falls back to VPN on failure.

options.text: A second NIC on each VM
options.explanation: Wrong. Doesn't provide WAN-link redundancy.
Wrong. Azure-to-Azure only, not on-prem failover.
Wrong. Outbound internet only.

questions.stem: You must alert when fewer than 2 healthy instances back a load balancer. Which signal type fits best?

options.text: A metric alert on the load balancer's Health Probe Status / Dip availability
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor metrics alert create` on the LB health-probe metric notifies when healthy backend count drops below threshold.
Wrong. Tracks control-plane ops, not live backend health.
Wrong. Per-VM console only.
Wrong. Cost-based, not health.

questions.stem: A storage account's blob soft delete is on, but a user permanently deleted a container. Can it be recovered, and how?

options.text: Yes, if container soft delete is enabled — undelete within the retention window
options.explanation: Correct. Container soft delete is separate from blob soft delete; with it on, `az storage container restore --name <c> --deleted-version <v>` recovers it.

options.text: No, soft delete never covers containers
options.explanation: Wrong. Container-level soft delete exists and must be enabled separately.

options.text: Only by restoring from a different account
options.explanation: Wrong. Not required when container soft delete is on.
Wrong. Locks don't restore deleted containers.

questions.stem: You must run a one-off batch job in a container, exiting when done, with per-second billing and no orchestrator. Which service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. ACI runs a container to completion with restartPolicy Never and per-second billing: `az container create --restart-policy Never ...`.
Wrong. Requires a managed cluster for a single job.
Wrong. Long-running web hosting, not a one-off container job.
Wrong. You'd manage and pay for the VM lifecycle yourself.

questions.stem: An internal-only API behind a load balancer must not be reachable from the internet. Which load balancer type do you use?

options.text: An internal (private) Standard Load Balancer with a private frontend IP
options.explanation: Correct. `az network lb create --frontend-ip ... --private-ip-address` keeps the frontend on a private VNet IP, never internet-exposed.
Wrong. Public frontend exposes it to the internet.
Wrong. Public-facing by default.
Wrong. DNS-based global routing, inherently public.

questions.stem: A deny assignment must stop even Owners from deleting resources in a managed resource group provisioned by a managed app. Where does this come from?

options.text: Azure-managed deny assignments created by the managed application / Blueprints
options.explanation: Correct. Deny assignments (distinct from role assignments) are created by Azure-managed scenarios and override Owner permissions; you don't author them directly via RBAC.

options.text: A custom RBAC role with deny
options.explanation: Wrong. RBAC roles can't express deny; only deny assignments do, and those are system-created.
Wrong. Locks prevent delete/modify but the question specifies deny assignments from managed apps.
Wrong. Policy denies non-compliant creates, not principal-scoped deny on existing resources.

questions.stem: You need near-real-time streaming of resource logs into a third-party SIEM. Which diagnostic destination should you choose?

options.explanation: Correct. Diagnostic settings stream to an Event Hub which SIEMs (Splunk, QRadar) consume in near-real-time. `az monitor diagnostic-settings create --event-hub ...`.
Wrong. Good for archive/batch, not low-latency streaming.
Wrong. Great for KQL but not the standard SIEM streaming ingress.
Wrong. Not a diagnostics destination.

questions.stem: You must give time-limited write access to a container that you can revoke instantly if leaked. What do you use?

options.text: A SAS tied to a stored access policy
options.explanation: Correct. The stored access policy lets you revoke or change the SAS by editing the policy — instant revocation without rotating account keys.

options.text: An ad-hoc SAS with a long expiry
options.explanation: Wrong. Ad-hoc SAS can't be revoked without rotating the account key.
Wrong. Full access, no scoping or quick revocation.
Wrong. Anyone could write; no control.

questions.stem: Provisioning must fail the deployment if a parameter value is outside an allowed list, before any resource is created. Which Bicep feature enforces this?

options.explanation: Correct. `@allowed(['dev','test','prod'])` validates parameters at compile/deploy time, failing fast before resource creation.
Wrong. Acts after resources exist.
Wrong. Network filtering, unrelated to parameter validation.
Wrong. Tags don't validate inputs.

questions.stem: DNS queries from your VNet must be forwarded to an on-prem DNS server for certain zones, and to Azure for others, without managing DNS VMs. What do you deploy?

options.text: Azure DNS Private Resolver with forwarding rulesets
options.explanation: Correct. The Private Resolver provides inbound/outbound endpoints and conditional forwarding rules to on-prem zones, fully managed — no DNS VMs.
Wrong. The requirement is to avoid managing DNS VMs.
Wrong. Public, no conditional forwarding to on-prem.
Wrong. Not scalable or dynamic.

questions.stem: An application registration needs permission to read user profiles via Microsoft Graph as the signed-in user. What do you configure?

options.text: A delegated Microsoft Graph permission (User.Read) with admin/user consent
options.explanation: Correct. In the app registration's API permissions, add the delegated `User.Read` Graph scope and grant consent; the app acts as the signed-in user.
Wrong. App-only acts without a user; the requirement says 'as the signed-in user' (delegated).
Wrong. A secret authenticates the app but doesn't grant Graph scopes.
Wrong. Azure RBAC doesn't govern Graph API scopes.

questions.stem: You want autoscale to also scale in safely without thrashing when load fluctuates near the threshold. Which setting addresses this?

options.text: A cool-down period between scale actions
options.explanation: Correct. The autoscale cooldown prevents rapid repeated scaling; set it in the autoscale profile so the system waits before the next action.

options.text: Lower the instance minimum to zero
options.explanation: Wrong. Doesn't address oscillation near a threshold.
Wrong. Vertical change, unrelated to scale thrashing.
Wrong. Then it never scales in.

questions.stem: Compliance requires that storage account keys cannot be used at all — only Entra ID auth. What do you set?

options.text: Disable shared key access on the storage account
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage account update --allow-shared-key-access false` blocks account-key and SAS-via-key auth, forcing Entra ID (RBAC) authorization.
Wrong. Keys still usable between rotations.
Wrong. Controls network path, not auth method.
Wrong. Unrelated to auth method.

questions.stem: A web app on App Service must scale out automatically based on HTTP queue length and CPU. Where do you configure the rules?

options.text: Autoscale settings on the App Service plan
options.explanation: Correct. Scale rules live at the plan level (the compute that hosts apps): `az monitor autoscale create --resource <plan>` with CPU/queue conditions.
Wrong. Scaling is a plan-level capability shared by apps on the plan.
Wrong. Unrelated resource.
Wrong. Locks don't scale.

questions.stem: Outbound SNAT port exhaustion is causing intermittent failures from many VMs sharing one load balancer. What's the recommended remedy?

options.text: Add a NAT gateway to the subnet for scalable outbound SNAT
options.explanation: Correct. NAT gateway provides far more SNAT ports and replaces LB outbound rules; `az network nat gateway create` then associate the subnet.

options.text: Add more public IPs to each VM
options.explanation: Wrong. Operationally messy and exposes VMs inbound.
Wrong. SNAT exhaustion isn't a compute-size issue.
Wrong. Aggressive timeouts can worsen reliability.

questions.stem: A break-glass emergency admin account must bypass Conditional Access MFA so admins aren't locked out if MFA fails tenant-wide. How do you handle it?

options.text: Exclude the dedicated break-glass account from CA policies and monitor its sign-ins
options.explanation: Correct. Microsoft recommends two excluded emergency accounts, tightly monitored with alerts on use, so a CA/MFA outage can't lock everyone out.

options.text: Give it no MFA via per-user settings
options.explanation: Wrong. Doesn't reliably bypass CA and isn't the documented pattern.
Wrong. Removes protection for everyone.

options.text: Share the global admin password broadly
options.explanation: Wrong. Severe security risk.

questions.stem: You need to see exactly who deleted a resource and when. Where do you look?

options.text: The Activity Log, filtered to the Delete operation on the resource
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor activity-log list --resource-id <id> --offset 7d` shows the caller, operation, and timestamp for control-plane deletes.
Wrong. Per-VM console only.
Wrong. Numeric telemetry, not who-did-what.
Wrong. Network flows, not management operations.

questions.stem: A premium file share needs to be backed up daily with 30-day retention. What protects it?

options.text: Azure Backup for Azure Files via a Recovery Services vault
options.explanation: Correct. `az backup protection enable-for-azurefileshare` with a policy of daily backups and 30-day retention snapshots the share.
Wrong. That's for blobs, not file-share backup.
Wrong. Blob-only and not a backup.
Wrong. Prevents deletion, doesn't back up.

questions.stem: You must guarantee a VM lands on dedicated physical hardware not shared with other customers for compliance. What do you provision?

options.text: An Azure Dedicated Host and place the VM on it
options.explanation: Correct. `az vm host create` provides single-tenant physical servers; deploy VMs onto the host for hardware isolation.
Wrong. Size doesn't guarantee single tenancy.
Wrong. Zones are about datacenter redundancy, not dedicated hardware.
Wrong. PPG co-locates VMs for latency; doesn't isolate hardware.

questions.stem: Two subnets in the same VNet must be isolated so they cannot talk to each other, while both still reach the internet. What do you apply?

options.text: NSGs on each subnet denying the other subnet's address range
options.explanation: Correct. Default intra-VNet allow lets subnets talk; add NSG deny rules for the peer subnet's CIDR while leaving internet egress allowed.
Wrong. Works but the question keeps them in one VNet; NSGs achieve isolation.
Wrong. Affects outbound internet, not inter-subnet isolation.
Wrong. Routing changes paths but NSGs enforce the deny.

questions.stem: Dynamic group membership should automatically include all users whose department equals 'Sales'. What do you configure?

options.text: A dynamic security group with a membership rule on the department attribute
options.explanation: Correct. `(user.department -eq "Sales")` as the dynamic rule auto-populates the group as user attributes change (requires Entra ID P1).

options.text: A static group with manual adds
options.explanation: Wrong. Not automatic as users change.
Wrong. CA gates sign-in, doesn't manage membership.
Wrong. AUs scope admin, not auto-membership by attribute.

questions.stem: A scheduled query rule must run every 5 minutes and fire when error log count exceeds 100. Which alert type do you build?

options.text: A log search (scheduled query) alert in Azure Monitor
options.explanation: Correct. `az monitor scheduled-query create` runs a KQL count query on a cadence and alerts when results cross the threshold.
Wrong. Metric alerts use platform metrics, not arbitrary log KQL counts.
Wrong. Limited to control-plane events.
Wrong. Cost only.

questions.stem: You must serve static website content (HTML/CSS/JS) directly from blob storage with a custom index document. What do you enable?

options.text: Static website hosting on the storage account
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage blob service-properties update --static-website --index-document index.html` serves the $web container over an HTTPS web endpoint.
Wrong. Makes it private, not a public website.
Wrong. SMB, not web hosting.
Wrong. Tiers blobs; doesn't host a site.

questions.stem: An AKS cluster must autoscale nodes when pods are unschedulable due to insufficient resources. What do you enable?

options.text: The cluster autoscaler on the node pool
options.explanation: Correct. `az aks update --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5` adds nodes when pods can't schedule and removes idle ones.
Wrong. HPA scales pods, not nodes; unschedulable pods still need more nodes.
Wrong. Doesn't auto-respond to pending pods.
Wrong. Not automatic.

questions.stem: A VM needs both a static public IP that persists and a DNS label like myapp.eastus.cloudapp.azure.com. What do you configure on the public IP?

options.text: A Standard static public IP with a DNS name label
options.explanation: Correct. `az network public-ip create --sku Standard --allocation-method Static --dns-name myapp` yields a persistent IP and an Azure FQDN.
Wrong. Dynamic IPs change and Basic is being retired.
Wrong. Private IPs aren't internet-reachable with a public FQDN.
Wrong. Provides a global DNS name but not a static VM public IP.

questions.stem: Hybrid identity: on-prem AD users must sign in to Azure with their existing password, with password hashes synced to the cloud. Which sync option?

options.text: Microsoft Entra Connect with Password Hash Synchronization
options.explanation: Correct. Entra Connect's PHS syncs a hash-of-the-hash to the cloud so users authenticate with the same password; simplest, most resilient hybrid option.
Wrong. Valid hybrid option but PHS is what 'hashes synced to the cloud' describes.
Wrong. Heavier, requires on-prem infra; not hash sync.
Wrong. Ignores the existing on-prem identities.

questions.stem: You must export cost data daily to a storage account for analysis in Power BI. What do you set up?

options.text: A scheduled export in Cost Management to a storage account
options.explanation: Correct. Cost Management exports push daily CSVs to storage: configure under Exports, then connect Power BI to the container.
Wrong. Diagnostic settings handle logs/metrics, not cost exports.
Wrong. Budgets alert on spend; they don't export data.
Wrong. Not a cost export mechanism.

questions.stem: A VM image with your hardened OS config must be reused across regions and subscriptions, versioned. What do you use?

options.text: Azure Compute Gallery (Shared Image Gallery) with image versions
options.explanation: Correct. `az sig image-version create` stores versioned images replicated to multiple regions and shareable across subscriptions.
Wrong. Snapshots aren't versioned, multi-region image distributables.

options.text: A storage blob of the VHD
options.explanation: Wrong. Manual and not integrated image lifecycle.
Wrong. For recovery, not image distribution.

questions.stem: Apps must reference Key Vault secrets that auto-update when rotated, without redeploying. What pattern do you use in App Service?

options.text: Key Vault references in app settings with a managed identity
options.explanation: Correct. `@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=...)` in app settings, resolved via the app's managed identity; rotating the secret reflects on next refresh without code changes.
Wrong. Requires redeploy on rotation and is insecure.

options.text: Store secrets in a config file
options.explanation: Wrong. Same redeploy/security problems.
Wrong. Unrelated to Key Vault secret references.

questions.stem: Diagnostics show asymmetric routing breaking a connection through an NVA. What Azure routing construct most likely needs adjustment?

options.text: User-defined routes (route tables) to ensure symmetric paths through the NVA
options.explanation: Correct. Asymmetric routing through firewalls/NVAs is fixed by aligning UDRs so request and response traverse the same appliance; review `az network route-table route list`.
Wrong. NSGs filter, they don't determine the forwarding path.
Wrong. Name resolution isn't the routing path.
Wrong. Unrelated to internal asymmetric routing.

questions.stem: You must delegate the ability to manage a specific subset of users (one region's staff) to a regional helpdesk, without tenant-wide rights. What scopes the admin?

options.text: An administrative unit containing those users, with a scoped role assignment
options.explanation: Correct. Place the region's users in an AU and assign the helpdesk a role scoped to that AU — admin rights apply only to its members.
Wrong. Tenant-wide, the opposite of scoped.
Wrong. Groups organize membership but don't scope admin roles by themselves.
Wrong. Unrelated to identity delegation.

questions.stem: Logs from multiple subscriptions must land in one central workspace governed by one team. What's the recommended architecture?

options.text: A centralized Log Analytics workspace with diagnostic settings/DCRs from each subscription pointing to it
options.explanation: Correct. Point each subscription's resources to the shared workspace; central team manages access and retention. Scales for governance.
Wrong. Fragments data and governance.
Wrong. No central KQL analysis.
Wrong. Per-VM console, not centralized logging.

questions.stem: A containerized API needs HTTPS ingress, revisions for blue-green, and scale-to-zero with minimal ops. Which service is the best fit?

options.explanation: Correct. Container Apps provides managed ingress with TLS, traffic-split revisions for blue-green, and KEDA scale-to-zero — minimal operational overhead.
Wrong. You manage TLS, scaling, and revisions yourself.
Wrong. Powerful but heavier ops than Container Apps for this requirement.
Wrong. Lacks the revision/scale-to-zero container model described.

questions.stem: You must prevent accidental data loss by recovering blobs deleted in the last 14 days. What do you enable?

options.text: Blob soft delete with a 14-day retention period
options.explanation: Correct. `az storage account blob-service-properties update --enable-delete-retention true --delete-retention-days 14` lets you undelete blobs within the window.
Wrong. Locks stop deletes but don't recover already-deleted blobs.
Wrong. Async copy, not deletion recovery.
Wrong. Tiers/expires blobs rather than recovering deletions.

questions.stem: BGP-learned routes from on-prem must propagate to a VNet's VMs over the VPN gateway. What must be enabled?

options.text: BGP on the VPN gateway and the local network gateway
options.explanation: Correct. Enable BGP with ASNs and peering IPs on both gateways (`az network vnet-gateway create --asn ...`) so routes exchange dynamically instead of static definitions.
Wrong. Unrelated to dynamic routing.
Wrong. NSGs filter, they don't propagate routes.
Wrong. Outbound SNAT only.

questions.stem: A user reports they can't be added to a role because the role is 'eligible' not 'active'. What product governs this state?

options.explanation: Correct. PIM introduces eligible assignments that users activate just-in-time; the user must activate the eligible role via the PIM portal or `az` PIM APIs.
Wrong. CA gates sign-in, not role eligibility/activation.
Wrong. Reviews recertify; they don't create the eligible state.
Wrong. Unrelated to role activation.
